perturbation.original_text: The Sub-Advisor shall maintain proper and complete records relating to the services to be provided under this Agreement for such period of time as may be required under Applicable Law, including (as applicable, in respect of the relevant Discretionary Funds) records with respect to the acquisition, holding and disposal of securities on behalf of the Funds, details of all brokers used and the aggregate dollar amount of brokerage commission paid in that regard to each broker.
perturbation.changed_text: The Sub-Advisor shall maintain proper and complete records relating to the services to be provided under this Agreement for a period of two years, including (as applicable, in respect of the relevant Discretionary Funds) records with respect to the acquisition, holding and disposal of securities on behalf of the Funds, details of all brokers used and the aggregate dollar amount of brokerage commission paid in that regard to each broker.
perturbation.explanation: Setting a fixed two-year record retention period may violate regulations that mandate longer retention periods for investment advisors.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 CFR § 275.204-2(e)(1) states that an investment adviser must preserve 'all books and records' for a period of not less than five years from the end of the fiscal year during which the last entry was made on such record. Specifying a two-year retention period directly contradicts this requirement.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 275.204-2 - Books and records to be maintained by investment advisers. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 275.204-2 - Books and records to be maintained by investment advisers. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 275.204-2 Books and records to be maintained by investment advisers. (a) Every investment adviser registered or required to be registered under section 203 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 80b-3 ) shall make and keep true, accurate and current the following books and records relating to its investment advisory business; (1) A journal or journals, including cash receipts and disbursements, records, and any other records of original entry forming the basis of entries in any ledger. (2) General and auxiliary ledgers (or other comparable records) reflecting asset, liability, reserve, capital, income and expense accounts. (3) A memorandum of each order given by the investment adviser for the purchase or sale of any security, of any instruction received by the investment adviser concerning the purchase, sale, receipt or delivery of a particular security, and of any modification or cancellation of any such order or instruction. Such memoranda shall show the terms and conditions of the order, instruction, modification or cancellation; shall identify the person connected with the investment adviser who recommended the transaction to the client and the person who placed such order; and shall show the account for which entered, the date of entry, and the bank, broker or dealer by or through whom executed where appropriate. Orders entered pursuant to the exercise of discretionary power shall be so designated. (4) All check books, bank statements, cancelled checks and cash reconciliations of the investment adviser. (5) All bills or statements (or copies thereof), paid or unpaid, relating to the business of the investment adviser as such. (6) All trial balances, financ
perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies a two-year record retention period, which directly contradicts the legal requirement stated in the Law Explanation and supported by the scraped snippets. The Law Explanation references 17 CFR § 275.204-2(e)(1), stating that investment advisors must preserve records for not less than five years. The 'Changed Text' reduces this period to two years, making it non-compliant and thus creating a legal contradiction.
